era_context: The mid-15th century was characterized by the final stages of the Hundred Years' War, the Council of Florence's ecumenical negotiations, the Ming Dynasty's consolidation, and the Ottoman Empire's expansion in the Eastern Mediterranean. This was a period of significant religious, political, and military transition across multiple civilizations.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Central Asia and Steppe regions (limited contemporary sources)

events.title: Council of Florence: Continued Ecumenical Negotiations
events.description: The Council of Florence continued its work in 1435 toward reconciliation between the Roman Catholic and Eastern Orthodox churches. Delegates from Constantinople were present, and negotiations were progressing toward a union agreement. The council was addressing fundamental theological differences, including the filioque controversy and liturgical practices. The negotiations would culminate in the union decree of 1439.
events.certainty_note: The council's proceedings are well-documented in official records.

events.title: Ming Dynasty: Xuande Emperor's Death
events.description: The Xuande Emperor of the Ming Dynasty died in 1435 (some sources date this to early 1436). His reign (1426–1435) was characterized by administrative consolidation, cultural flourishing, and the continuation of the Zheng He voyages, which were winding down. The emperor's death marked a transition to a regency period under his young son, the Zhengtong Emperor, who was only 8 or 9 years old. This succession would lead to increased eunuch influence at court.
key_figures: Zhengtong Emperor (successor, child during this period)
events.certainty_note: The Xuande Emperor's death and succession are well-documented in Ming official records, though the exact date varies slightly between sources (1435 or early 1436).
cross_references: Leads to regency period and eventual Tumu Crisis of 1449

events.title: Hundred Years' War: Treaty of Arras
events.description: The Treaty of Arras was signed in September 1435, ending the alliance between England and Burgundy during the Hundred Years' War. The treaty was negotiated between France and Burgundy, with Burgundy agreeing to withdraw from its alliance with England. This was a major diplomatic victory for France, as it isolated England and strengthened France's position in the war. The treaty marked a turning point in the Hundred Years' War, as England's position in France would continue to deteriorate after this date.
key_figures: Philip III, Duke of Burgundy (Burgundian ruler)
Henry VI (King of England, child during this period)

sources.name: Jean de Wavrin, Recueil des croniques et anchiennes istories de la Grant Bretaigne

events.certainty_note: The Treaty of Arras is well-documented in official records and contemporary chronicles. The date (September 1435) is firmly established.
cross_references: Leads to English loss of most French territories by 1453

events.title: Portuguese Exploration: Continued African Expeditions
events.description: Portuguese explorers, sponsored by Prince Henry the Navigator, continued systematic exploration of the West African coast in 1435. These expeditions were gathering information about African geography, trade routes, and indigenous peoples. The voyages were motivated by desires for gold, spices, and knowledge of African kingdoms. Portuguese caravels were becoming more sophisticated, allowing for longer voyages and better navigation.

sources.name: Gomes de Zurara, Chronicle of the Discovery and Conquest of Guinea

events.certainty_note: Portuguese exploration of the African coast was ongoing during this period, but specific events for 1435 are not always precisely dated in sources.
cross_references: Part of Portuguese Age of Exploration (1415–1522)

events.title: Ottoman Empire: Consolidation in the Balkans
events.description: The Ottoman Empire under Murad II continued its consolidation of power in the Balkans. By 1435, Ottoman control extended over much of the former Serbian and Bulgarian territories. The Ottomans were establishing administrative systems and military garrisons throughout the region. This period saw the integration of Balkan territories into the Ottoman administrative structure.

sources.name: Ottoman court records and administrative documents

events.certainty_note: Ottoman expansion in the Balkans during this period is well-attested, though specific events for 1435 are not always precisely dated.
cross_references: Leads to Ottoman conquest of Constantinople (1453)

disconfirming_evidence: The exact date of the Xuande Emperor's death is disputed between sources, with some placing it in 1435 and others in early 1436. This creates uncertainty about the precise year of the Zhengtong Emperor's succession. The Treaty of Arras is sometimes dated to September 1435 and sometimes to 1436 in different sources, though 1435 is more commonly accepted.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1435 is moderate but unevenly distributed. European sources are relatively abundant, particularly for political and military events. Chinese sources are rich but require specialized knowledge. The historical record is shaped by the survival of written chronicles and official records, which tend to emphasize political and military events. There is a significant Western and European bias in available English-language sources.

graph_edges.note: The negotiations of 1435 led to the union decree of 1439
The Xuande Emperor's death and the succession of the young Zhengtong Emperor led to increased eunuch influence and eventually the Tumu Crisis of 1449
The Treaty of Arras isolated England and contributed to the end of the Hundred Years' War in 1453
